perturbation.original_text: (a) Franchise Owner's Right to Renew. Subject to the provisions of subparagraph 2.6(b) below, and if you have substantially complied with all provisions of this Agreement and all other agreements between us, on expiration of the Initial Term, if you refurbish and decorate the Premises, replace fixtures, furnishings, wall decor, furniture, equipment, and signs and otherwise modify the Franchise in compliance with specifications and standards then applicable under new or renewal franchises for The Joint Corp. Location franchises, you will have the right to renew the Franchise for one (1) additional term of ten (10) years (the "Renewal Term").
perturbation.changed_text: (a) Franchise Owner's Right to Renew. Subject to the provisions of subparagraph 2.6(b) below, and if you have substantially complied with all provisions of this Agreement and all other agreements between us, on expiration of the Initial Term, if you refurbish and decorate the Premises, replace fixtures, furnishings, wall decor, furniture, equipment, and signs and otherwise modify the Franchise in compliance with specifications and standards then applicable under new or renewal franchises for The Joint Corp. Location franchises, you will have the option to renew the Franchise for one (1) additional term of one (1) year (the "Renewal Term").
perturbation.explanation: The original text grants a 10-year renewal term, whereas the modification reduces it to 1 year, potentially conflicting with franchise laws that protect franchisee renewal rights to terms of reasonable length.
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 U.S.C. § 2803 concerns the renewal of franchise agreements and the circumstances under which a franchisor may fail to renew a franchise. By reducing the franchise renewal term to one year, the modified text may not provide a 'reasonable' period to allow the franchisee to recoup their investments and realize a return, particularly if significant capital expenditures were required which goes against the implied principles of good faith and fair dealing.

Trial and interim franchises (a) Nonapplicability of statutory nonrenewal provisions The provisions of section 2802 of this title shall not apply to the nonrenewal of any franchise relationship- (1) under a trial franchise; or (2) under an interim franchise. (b) Definitions For purposes of this section- (1) The term "trial franchise" means any franchise- (A) which is entered into on or after June 19, 1978; (B) the franchisee of which has not previo
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the franchise renewal term from ten years to one year. The Law Explanation states that this reduction 'may not provide a 'reasonable' period to allow the franchisee to recoup their investments and realize a return, particularly if significant capital expenditures were required which goes against the implied principles of good faith and fair dealing.' While 15 U.S.C. § 2803 primarily concerns the nonrenewal of franchises under specific circumstances (trial and interim franchises), its citation in the Law Explanation highlights the broader legal concern about the reasonableness of franchise terms and the protection of franchisee investments. The alteration therefore creates a potential legal contradiction, as reducing the renewal term could be deemed unreasonable, undermining the franchisee's opportunity to recoup investment, according to the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation. This falls under 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' because the modified term is potentially inconsistent with franchise laws safeguarding franchisee renewal rights.

perturbation.original_text: (c) Renewal Agreement; Releases. Should you choose to renew the Franchise, you must provide us with written notice of that intent no earlier than two (2) years and no later than one (1) year before the expiration of the Initial Term.
perturbation.changed_text: (c) Renewal Agreement; Releases. Should you choose to renew the Franchise, you must provide us with written notice of that intent no earlier than five (5) years and no later than four (4) years before the expiration of the Initial Term.
perturbation.explanation: By extending the notification window for renewal intention to 4-5 years prior to expiration, the modification imposes an unreasonably early deadline, conflicting with franchise regulations requiring reasonable notification periods.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Franchise Relations Act, CA Bus. & Prof. Code § 20025
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 20025 states that it is unlawful for a franchise to be terminated before the expiration, except for good cause. Requiring notice 4-5 years in advance is not 'reasonable' because business environments, personal circumstances, and market conditions can change greatly, therefore a franchisee may not be in a position to make an educated decision so far in advance.

Franchise Relations [20000 - 20044] ( Chapter 5.5 added by Stats. 1980, Ch. 1355, Sec. 1. ) ARTICLE 4. Nonrenewal [20025 - 20026] ( Article 4 added by Stats. 1980, Ch. 1355, Sec. 1. ) 20025. No franchisor may fail to renew a franchise unless such franchisor provides the franchisee at least 180 days prior written notice of its intention not to renew; and (a) During the 180 days prior to expiration of the franchise the franchisor permits the franchisee to sell his business to a purchaser meeting the franchisor’s then current requirements for granting new franchises, or if the franchisor is not granting a significant number of new franchises, the then current requirements for granting renewal franchises; or (b) (1) The refusal to renew is not for the purpose of converting the franchisee’s business premises to operation by employees or agents of the franchisor for such franchisor’s own account, provided, that nothing in this paragraph shall prohibit a franchisor from exercising a right of first refusal to purchase the franchisee’s business; and (2) Upon expiration of the franchise, the franchisor agrees not to seek t
perturbation.justification: The changed text imposes a renewal notification window of 4-5 years prior to the expiration of the franchise, which contradicts the principle of 'reasonable' notice as discussed in the Law Explanation. While the provided scraped law snippet (BPC 20025) specifies a minimum notice period of 180 days for non-renewal by the franchisor, it implies that franchisees also require a reasonable timeframe to make informed decisions. The modification's extended notification period could be considered unreasonable because it forces franchisees to make decisions far in advance, potentially undermining their ability to assess future business conditions. This aligns with the definition of 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction' where a time-sensitive obligation does not align with legal requirements.

perturbation.original_text: (c) Unless we agree otherwise, you must open your franchise for business no later than nine (9) months from the Effective Date of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: (c) Unless we agree otherwise, you must open your franchise for business no later than two (2) months from the Effective Date of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text allows nine months to open the franchise, whereas the modification reduces it to two months. This may violate state franchise laws related to giving the franchisee reasonable time to establish the business.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Wisconsin Fair Dealership Law, Wis. Stat. § 135.03
perturbation.law_explanation: Wisconsin Statute 135.03 relates to the termination or alteration of dealerships (which can include franchises) and requires good cause for such actions. While this section concerns termination, requiring a franchise to open within two months may be considered an unreasonable burden and lack of good cause for demanding an opening so quickly, effectively setting the franchisee up to fail from the beginning. Two months may not be sufficient to obtain financing, permits, real estate, and staffing.

Instead, a dealership must either put those symbols to such use that the public associates the dealer with the trademark or prominently display the logo as an implicit guarantee of quality. Such use by a dealership ties its fortunes to the reputation of the grantor, giving the grantor superior bargaining power that the grantor might use to exploit the dealer. perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the original contract by reducing the time to open a franchise from nine months to two months. The Law Explanation states that Wisconsin Statute 135.03, while related to termination, could be interpreted as finding the two-month requirement an 'unreasonable burden'. The Perturbation Explanation suggests the change violates state franchise laws related to reasonable time to establish a business. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references Wisconsin Statute 135.02, and discusses dealerships; however, it does not mention timelines or reasonable time to establish the business. The modification creates a potentially unreasonable deadline, and could be deemed a violation, thus creating a contradiction.
